title: Variables & Types
subtitle: Give the computer little labeled boxes to remember things for you.
goal: Create variables in Java, pick the right type for the job (int, double, char, boolean, and friends), reassign and print them, and meet the handy var keyword.

blocks.heading: What is a variable? 📦
blocks.html:
A variable is like a labeled box where the computer keeps something for you. You write a name on the box (like age), put a value inside (like 11), and later you can peek inside just by saying the name.

The cool part: you can change what's in the box whenever you want, and the computer always knows what's inside.

blocks.heading: Java likes to know the type 🏷️
blocks.html:
Java is statically typed. That's a fancy way of saying: every box has to say what kind of thing it holds before you put anything in it. A box for whole numbers can't suddenly hold text.

You declare a variable like this: int age = 11; — first the type (int), then the name (age), then = and the value. Every line ends with a semicolon ;.

blocks.heading: The main types you'll use
blocks.html:
These are Java's primitive types — the basic building blocks:

  • int — a whole number, like 7 or -3
  • double — a number with a decimal point, like 1.45
  • char — a single character in single quotes, like 'A'
  • boolean — either true or false

There are a few more for special cases: long (really big whole numbers), float (a smaller decimal number), and byte/short (tiny numbers). You'll mostly reach for int and double for now.

blocks.caption: Four boxes, four types
blocks.output:
Age: 11
Height in meters: 1.45
Grade: A
Likes pizza? true
blocks.explain: Each line makes a box, labels it, and fills it. The + glues the text to the value so it prints together. Notice 'A' uses single quotes because it's a char (one character), while text in System.out.println uses double quotes.

blocks.html: Numbers, not text! Write int age = 11; with no quotes. If you wrote "11" with quotes, Java would think it's text, not a number you can add or compare.

blocks.heading: Naming your boxes (camelCase) 🐫
blocks.html:
You pick the names, but Java programmers follow a habit called camelCase: start lowercase, and bump up each new word like a camel's hump — myPetAge, favoriteColor, topScore.

Names should describe what's inside. int x = 11; works, but int age = 11; tells the next person what the box means. Names can't have spaces and can't start with a number.

blocks.heading: Reassigning: change what's in the box
blocks.html: Once a box exists, you can put a new value in it — without writing the type again. You only say the type the first time you create the box.

blocks.caption: The same box, changed twice
blocks.explain: First line declares the box with int. After that we just say score = ... to refill it. The clever line score = score + 5 means 'take what's in score, add 5, and put it back' — that's how 25 becomes 30.

blocks.question: Which line correctly creates an int box named age holding the value 12?
blocks.explain: The pattern is type, then name, then = value, then a semicolon: int age = 12;. The quotes in "12" would make it text, not a number.

blocks.question: You already wrote int score = 10;. How do you change score to 25 on the next line?
blocks.explain: You only write the type (int) the first time. After that, just score = 25; refills the same box. Writing int again would try to make a brand new box with the same name, which confuses Java.

blocks.heading: The var shortcut (Java 10+) ✨
blocks.html:
Typing the type every time gets repetitive. Since Java 10 you can write var for variables inside a method, and Java figures out the type for you from the value. This is called type inference.

var age = 12; becomes an int automatically, because 12 is a whole number. var name = "Maya"; becomes text. It's still strongly typed — Java just saved you some typing.

blocks.caption: Letting Java guess the type with var
blocks.output:
Maya is 12 years old.
A snack costs $4.5
blocks.explain: Java sees "Maya" and makes name text, sees 12 and makes age an int, sees 4.50 and makes price a double. Notice 4.50 prints as 4.5 — a double drops the extra zero at the end because it means the same number.

blocks.html: var only works inside methods (like inside main) and only when you give it a value right away, so Java can tell the type. var x; with no value won't work — Java has nothing to guess from.

blocks.question: What type does Java give the variable in var lucky = 7;?
options: double, because all numbers are decimals
String, because it's in a var
int, because 7 is a whole number
char, because it's a single digit
blocks.explain: var looks at the value to decide the type. 7 is a whole number with no decimal point, so Java picks int.

blocks.title: Make a pet box
blocks.instructions: Create a String variable called petName with your pet's name (or a made-up one), and an int variable called petAge with its age. Then print a sentence using both. Press Check when it runs.
blocks.solutionOutput: My pet is Rex and is 3 years old.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — two boxes, two types, one sentence!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you keep a String petName and an int petAge, and print them with System.out.println.

blocks.title: Add up the fruit
blocks.instructions: There are 4 apples and 5 oranges. Make an int variable called total that holds apples + oranges, then print it. The answer should be 9.
blocks.passMsg: 9 fruit — you can do math with variables now!
blocks.failMsg: Set total to apples + oranges (not a fixed number), so Java does the adding for you.

blocks.title: Practice variables on LeetCode & Codeforces
blocks.intro: Beginner problems to lock in what you learned.

challenge.title: Build your own ID card
challenge.instructions: Make four variables that describe a person (real or made-up): a String for name, an int for age, a double for height in meters, and a boolean for whether they can code. Print each one on its own line, like a little ID card.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Name: Sam
Age: 12
Height: 1.5
Can code: true
challenge.hint: Pick the right type for each fact: text goes in a String, whole numbers in an int, decimals in a double, and yes/no in a boolean. Use one System.out.println per line.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 A full ID card with four different types — you've mastered variables! Day 2 complete.
